title: Groundbreaking

jokes.text: Their response was no. [Yale rejected the library]
jokes.setup: Selina's team is eagerly awaiting Yale's response about hosting her presidential library
jokes.punchline: Blunt, flat delivery: 'Their response was no.'
jokes.explanation: The brutal economy of 'Their response was no' after implied expectation of good news is textbook Veep deflation comedy. Short, sharp, and well-placed as an episode opener.

jokes.text: Freeing Tibet was a monumental achievement. Yes, it was. That was a success despite an almost pathological level of incompetence.
jokes.setup: Leon West calls Selina's Tibet achievement 'monumental'
jokes.punchline: He immediately qualifies it as succeeding 'despite an almost pathological level of incompetence'
jokes.explanation: The backhanded compliment structure is clean — builds up then immediately undercuts. The phrase 'pathological level of incompetence' is vivid and specific enough to land well.

jokes.text: Her biased discretion speaks well of her.
jokes.setup: Praise for Selina continues on the news segment
jokes.punchline: Calling something 'biased discretion' as a compliment is a self-contradicting, absurd non-compliment
jokes.explanation: An odd, internally contradictory compliment that functions as a veiled insult — classic Veep. Slightly too throwaway to register fully but sharp on the page.

jokes.text: You look absolutely radiant. Oh, thank you! Yes. Your stay in the insane asylum, it's really agreed with you. Oh. It was a spa.
jokes.setup: Andrew compliments Selina on looking radiant after her mental health stay
jokes.punchline: He calls it an 'insane asylum'; she corrects with 'it was a spa' — a running denial that becomes its own joke
jokes.explanation: The 'insane asylum' drop is perfectly timed after the flattery setup, and Selina's flat 'It was a spa' denial sets up a running gag. Character-specific: Andrew would be the one to say this with a charming smile.

jokes.text: So what do you think? Three Meyer-sketeers hang a shingle?
jokes.setup: Andrew pitches a partnership to Selina and a colleague
jokes.punchline: 'Three Meyer-sketeers' — a self-aggrandizing pun folding Selina's name into the Three Musketeers
jokes.explanation: The pun is groan-worthy in a deliberate Andrew way — he's the kind of man who would make this pun and think himself charming. Character-integrated but not particularly sharp.

jokes.text: Jonah! I'm pulling all my financial support. I am pulling this creature right off the congressional ballot and replacing him with his cousin Ezra.
jokes.setup: Furlong addresses Jonah
jokes.punchline: Jonah is called 'this creature' and is being replaced by a random cousin — reducing him to a disposable political prop
jokes.explanation: 'This creature' is perfectly Furlong — his contempt for Jonah is always specific and dehumanizing. The replacement by cousin Ezra escalates the humiliation beautifully.

jokes.text: God, I really thought we were gonna win Guam.
jokes.setup: A speech lists the major states where Selina lost her primary campaign
jokes.punchline: Amidst losing Ohio, Florida, Texas, etc., someone laments specifically losing Guam — the most irrelevant territory listed
jokes.explanation: Perfectly constructed: the list builds to Guam as the ultimate political irrelevance, then someone singling it out as their specific disappointment is wonderfully absurd. The economy is flawless — one short line delivers the entire joke. One of the episode's sharper moments.

jokes.text: Jesus, I need to get drunk and slop-fuck an intern.
jokes.setup: Selina has just suspended her campaign in a public speech
jokes.punchline: Her immediate private reaction is graphically self-destructive and vulgar — the perfect Selina response to political failure
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional character comedy. 'Slop-fuck an intern' is a phrase only Selina Meyer could say — it's vulgar, desperate, politically on-brand (intern seduction as presidential cliché), and delivered mid-concession speech. The juxtaposition of public dignity and private dissolution is the core Veep joke, executed perfectly here. One of the episode's standout lines.

jokes.text: Jonie, look, I have a special treat... [box is revealed to be empty] See, the box is empty to show that all you need to be happy is right here. Well, that's stupid. Yeah, it was a waste of $30.
jokes.setup: Richard brings Jonah a 'special treat' in a box to cheer him up
jokes.punchline: The box is empty — Richard's earnest explanation of the metaphor is immediately punctured by Jonah calling it stupid, then Richard agreeing it was a waste of $30
jokes.explanation: Three-beat perfection: (1) the absurd gift setup, (2) Richard's sincere philosophical explanation, (3) immediate self-deflation acknowledging it cost $30. Richard's character — earnest to the point of absurdity — is perfectly deployed. The '$30' detail is genius specificity. One of the episode's best jokes.

jokes.text: In two years, I'm gonna run against my motard cousin, Ezra, who's been a congressman for, like, two days and he's already passed, like, 10 bills.
jokes.setup: Jonah dismisses the idea of waiting two years to reclaim his seat
jokes.punchline: His attempt to insult Ezra as incompetent backfires — Ezra has already been more effective as a congressman in days than Jonah was ever
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Jonah's complete lack of self-awareness — insulting Ezra while inadvertently demonstrating that Ezra is already a better congressman. 'Motard' is a Jonah-specific insult coinage that adds character flavor.

jokes.text: Well, two years is a long time. I mean, you could petition for a recall if they weren't unconstitutional. Can I petition for a recall against Ezra? Unequivocally, no. That's a great idea. That's awesome.
jokes.setup: Richard mentions recalls are unconstitutional
jokes.punchline: Jonah immediately asks if he can do the unconstitutional thing; Richard says no; Jonah hears 'great idea'
jokes.explanation: Classic Jonah stupidity comedy — he processes 'no' as 'yes' without pause. The structure is clean and the character specificity is high: only Jonah would respond to an explicit constitutional prohibition with 'that's awesome.'

jokes.text: I'll let you be Aquaman. No tidal wave.
jokes.setup: Jonah, a disgraced adult ex-congressman, invites Richard to hang out
jokes.punchline: He's offering to play a children's superhero game with specific rules — the infantilism of the situation is the joke
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'No tidal wave' as a house rule reveals Jonah is clearly the one who normally plays Aquaman, which deepens the pathos-comedy. His regression to childhood after political humiliation is sharp.

jokes.text: Does a rabbit need a yearly vaccination for myxomatosis? It does. Well, maybe. It depends on the rabbit. But, yes.
jokes.setup: A random rabbit vaccination question is asked mid-scene
jokes.punchline: Richard's answer is confidently wrong, then uncertain, then somehow arrives back at yes — his characteristic pseudo-expertise
jokes.explanation: Richard's confident meandering non-answer is perfectly in character. The rabbit vaccination question arriving from nowhere is wonderfully Veep — mundane specificity landing in absurd context.

jokes.text: Mom, can Richard stay the night? Can I please, Mrs. Ryan? Oh, sure! I'll make waffles for breakfast. Mom, I don't want waffles! Okay, then I won't make waffles.
jokes.setup: Jonah asks his mom if Richard can stay over like a child
jokes.punchline: The whole exchange plays as a child's sleepover, with Jonah immediately having a tantrum about waffles — a 40-year-old congressman in full regression
jokes.explanation: Jonah's infantilism is a reliable comic engine and this scene maximizes it — adult man, failed congressman, still fighting with mom about waffles. The waffle tantrum is the exact right absurd specific detail.

jokes.text: My great-grandfather bought it for her with the money he made from his first major arms deal. Wow. Well, we'll have to see what I look like later wearing nothing but blood diamonds.
jokes.setup: Jaffar presents Selina with a romantic heirloom ring with a loving backstory
jokes.punchline: Selina's response is to cheekily embrace the arms-dealer-blood-diamond provenance in the most Selina way — morally unbothered, sexually playful
jokes.explanation: 'Wearing nothing but blood diamonds' is a top-tier Selina line — it's simultaneously seductive, politically corrupt, and self-aware in the most Selina Meyer way possible. The setup (arms deal provenance) creates the opportunity and she takes it perfectly. High quotability, strong character integration.

jokes.text: It's the woman who freed Tibet! [Shouted by a passerby to Selina on the street]
jokes.setup: Selina is being mobbed by press about Montez's declining numbers
jokes.punchline: Someone shouts about Tibet as if that's her singular defining achievement — the Tibet thing as Selina's ironic legacy marker
jokes.explanation: Functional running gag — Tibet as Selina's accidentally monumental legacy is a recurring joke. Here it's quick shorthand rather than a developed beat.

jokes.text: How come you never talk about your stay in Arizona? Oh, I love the Grand Canyon. That's not an answer. Look over here! Arizona? Yeah, it was a spa. It was just a spa.
jokes.setup: Reporter asks about Selina's stay in Arizona (the mental health facility)
jokes.punchline: Selina's deflection via Grand Canyon non-sequitur, Gary's frantic redirection, and the now-ritual 'it was a spa' denial
jokes.explanation: The running gag escalates naturally here — now reporters are asking directly. Gary's panic and Selina's non-answer ('I love the Grand Canyon') are both character-true. The 'it was a spa' repetition is earning its keep as a running gag.

jokes.text: You were here yesterday. That's right! They love you so much, they can't keep away. Ma'am, that was a month ago. Gary is humoring you.
jokes.setup: Selina visits her mother in the care facility and Gary says 'They love you so much, they can't keep away'
jokes.punchline: Marjorie flatly corrects: that was a month ago, Gary is humoring you
jokes.explanation: Marjorie's deadpan factual correction of Gary's enabling behavior is perfectly in character — she's the only person around Selina who refuses to manage her ego. 'Gary is humoring you' is a devastating little truth bomb.

jokes.text: Gary, are you sure that this isn't too much medication? People think we're married. I think it's the perfect amount.
jokes.setup: Selina questions if her mother is over-medicated
jokes.punchline: Gary's response — 'I think it's the perfect amount' — implies the medication is for Selina's benefit, not her mother's. Also the non-sequitur 'people think we're married' as ambient comic noise.
jokes.explanation: Gary's 'perfect amount' is a dark little line — he's implicitly managing Selina's family for her. The 'people think we're married' line as ambient commentary adds texture.

jokes.text: Gary's response to the marriage comment: 'I think it's the perfect amount.' (referring to medication)
jokes.setup: Selina asks Gary if she's on too much medication
jokes.punchline: Gary enthusiastically endorses the medication level — implying Selina is more manageable when medicated, a dark flip on his usual servility
jokes.explanation: Dark little joke — Gary's 'perfect amount' is an honest self-interested assessment. The reversal of his usual total subservience makes it work.

jokes.text: As soon as I heard what a vulnerable state you were in, I got on the first flight. Hi, Andrew. Okay, I'm gonna get the orderly with the big needles. Let's not do that.
jokes.setup: Andrew shows up at the mental health facility, claiming concern
jokes.punchline: Selina's immediate response is to summon 'the orderly with the big needles' for Andrew — her contempt weaponized
jokes.explanation: Selina's instant power play — offering to have Andrew sedated rather than engage with him — is perfect character comedy. The 'Let's not do that' understatement from someone (presumably Gary) adds a nice capper.

jokes.text: He's like a bald dog with a bone.
jokes.setup: Selina complains that Leon West won't let the spa story go
jokes.punchline: 'Bald dog with a bone' — a mangled idiom that also references Leon's baldness
jokes.explanation: A well-crafted mixed metaphor that doubles as a physical description — the idiom substitution is pointed. Selina's mixed idioms are a recurring character tic, and this one is better constructed than most.

jokes.text: Well, ma'am, a buddy of mine works at the 'Post' snack shop. He says Leon's on the outs. The editors think he's lost all sense of objectivity, and he's eating Luna bars for women.
jokes.setup: Mike claims to have insider intel on Leon West from his Washington Post source
jokes.punchline: His 'source' is someone at the snack shop; the evidence that Leon is unhinged is that he eats Luna bars 'for women'
jokes.explanation: Two-tier joke: Mike's 'source' being the snack shop worker is a perfect character beat (his institutional access is hilariously marginal), and 'he's eating Luna bars for women' as evidence of unhinged behavior is absurdly specific and very funny. Pure Mike McLintock comedy.

jokes.text: He's like this demented creep who follows me around and gets all in my personal life and thinks about me 24-7, nonstop. Cuckoo!
jokes.setup: Selina describes Leon West as a deranged stalker
jokes.punchline: The description perfectly describes Selina's own obsessive relationship with media coverage — she's describing herself
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony lands well — her description of Leon's obsession with her is indistinguishable from her obsession with coverage. 'Cuckoo!' as a self-satisfied exclamation while being completely unself-aware is sharp.

jokes.text: And so now the staircase goes right up and smashes through the glass ceiling. Exactly what it's like to be a woman.
jokes.setup: The new library design has a staircase that smashes through a glass ceiling
jokes.punchline: Selina notes it's exactly what it's like to be a woman — then the architect adds that it's unstable, which Selina also agrees is like being a woman
jokes.explanation: The double beat (glass ceiling + instability both 'like being a woman') is well-structured. The second application of the logic is the stronger moment. Slightly on-the-nose politically but executed with good rhythm.

jokes.text: It looks like a vagina, ma'am. See? Okay, that's from an expert.
jokes.setup: Everyone is admiring the new library model
jokes.punchline: Marjorie, deadpan, says it looks like a vagina. Selina's 'that's from an expert' nod to Marjorie's lesbianism is the second beat.
jokes.explanation: Marjorie's flat expert delivery makes this. Selina's 'that's from an expert' callback to Marjorie's sexuality is quick and well-timed. The joke sets up several minutes of vagina-architecture comedy.

jokes.text: What if you trim the topiary around the edge? That could help. Nobody's gonna trim the topiary. I'm not a porn star.
jokes.setup: Discussion of how to fix the vaginal resemblance of the library design
jokes.punchline: 'Nobody's gonna trim the topiary. I'm not a porn star.' — Selina refusing architectural grooming advice as a personal affront
jokes.explanation: Selina treating architectural landscaping as a personal grooming choice is absurdly funny and entirely character-specific. 'I'm not a porn star' as the dignified exit from the argument is perfectly Selina.

jokes.text: You could be, but you chose politics.
jokes.setup: Selina says 'I'm not a porn star'
jokes.punchline: 'You could be, but you chose politics' — implying she had viable porn star options but made the nobler choice
jokes.explanation: Tight comeback, excellent economy. The implication is both a compliment and an insult simultaneously — classic Veep double-edged wit.

jokes.text: What is this, the clitoris? You know what would be funny? We should put the men's bathroom there. They'll never find it.
jokes.setup: Continuing examination of the vagina-shaped library model
jokes.punchline: 'Put the men's bathroom there — they'll never find it' — weaponizing the anatomy joke for feminist commentary
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely well-constructed joke — 'they'll never find it' is both anatomically observational and a political comment about men's relationship to female pleasure, delivered casually by a former president. Economy is excellent. One of the episode's best lines.

jokes.text: Well, actually, ma'am, the female pleasure center is quite extensive. Ah. Uh, no, it's not. And that's the crypt, ma'am. Right, that's what I just said. No, the crypt. What?
jokes.setup: Marjorie provides anatomical clarification about the clitoris; staff member points out the crypt
jokes.punchline: Selina mishears 'crypt' as something else, then has to process that her library contains her future burial site
jokes.explanation: The escalation from sex joke to death is a classic Veep tonal pivot. Selina's confusion (mishearing 'crypt') and then the horror of realizing her library contains her tomb is a wonderful beat. 'Right, that's what I just said' before understanding is good comic momentum.

jokes.text: I'm gonna be buried there? Yeah, most of the formers are buried at their libraries.
jokes.setup: Selina has just realized the library model includes a crypt
jokes.punchline: The staff member's casual 'yeah, most of the formers are buried at their libraries' treats presidential burial as a known quirk — Selina's horrified 'I gotta...'
jokes.explanation: The deadpan delivery of burial as routine library feature is funny, and Selina's existential crisis is well-played. The 'I gotta...' as a trail-off response is a nice underplayed reaction.

jokes.text: See, that wouldn't have happened if I paid you more. More? You never... Now, ma'am, there is a problem.
jokes.setup: Mike mentions his daughter got into a good school with financial aid
jokes.punchline: Selina says that wouldn't have happened 'if I paid you more' — flipping the congratulations into a self-serving comment. Mike starts to protest but pivots to work.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Selina — she manages to make Mike's daughter's scholarship about herself and her stinginess. Mike's starting-to-object-then-capitulating 'More? You never—' is a good character beat.

jokes.text: I'm not gonna have my vagi-brary underground railroaded by this.
jokes.setup: Discussion of the slave quarters discovered under the library site
jokes.punchline: 'Vagi-brary' as Selina's portmanteau for the vagina-shaped library, plus 'underground railroaded' inadvertently referencing the Underground Railroad while discussing slave history
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's comedic peak. 'Vagi-brary' is an instant classic coinage — it names the running vagina-library joke perfectly. Then 'underground railroaded' (vs. 'railroaded') is either a masterful Freudian slip or a deliberate double entendre that references the Underground Railroad while discussing slave quarters under the library. The density of comedy per syllable is extraordinary. Character-specific: only Selina would coin 'vagi-brary' and be completely oblivious to the railroad pun. Genuinely exceptional writing.

jokes.text: But he's Italian, so he knows how to keep bodies buried.
jokes.setup: They're trying to keep quiet about the slave quarters discovery; only the site manager knows
jokes.punchline: Mike's ethnic stereotype deployed specifically to argue for the site manager's discretion about 'bodies buried' — the mafia reference in a slavery context is darkly absurd
jokes.explanation: The double meaning of 'bodies buried' in the context of slave remains is darkly clever. The Italian stereotype is fairly lazy but the specific application here (to justify silence about an atrocity) has a Veep-appropriate dark edge.

jokes.text: Slaves, Mother? ... Okay, Cat... what room is this, Catherine? ... I mean, when did we build an Indian casino gift shop? It's the nursery. Seriously? My God. Well, where are the toys? Or is the baby gonna play chess against death?
jokes.setup: Catherine confronts Selina about the slave quarters; Selina pivots to insult the nursery decor
jokes.punchline: 'Indian casino gift shop' for the nursery decor, then 'play chess against death' for the lack of toys — two perfectly-aimed insults that escalate the comedy while deflecting the moral issue
jokes.explanation: 'Play chess against death' is an extraordinary image — it's a Bergman reference delivered as casual nursery criticism. 'Indian casino gift shop' is vivid and specific. Both phrases are quotable, both reveal Selina using aesthetic snobbery to dodge moral accountability. One of the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: Listen to me, Catherine Kinte...
jokes.setup: Selina is about to lecture Catherine about slave history comparisons
jokes.punchline: Calling Catherine 'Catherine Kinte' — a reference to Kunta Kinte from 'Roots' — as Selina tries to win an argument about whether women's oppression is worse than slavery
jokes.explanation: The 'Catherine Kinte' name substitution is perfectly economical — it immediately signals Selina is about to say something outrageously inappropriate. The reference is specific enough to land and character-appropriate: Selina's casual racism is always in service of self-interest.

jokes.text: Okay, so you wanna talk about second-class citizens? You wanna do that? Blacks got the vote in 1870. When did women get the vote? 1920! Sistas!
jokes.setup: Selina tries to argue that women were more oppressed than Black Americans
jokes.punchline: She wraps the argument with 'Sistas!' — a patronizing attempted solidarity that makes it worse
jokes.explanation: Selina using voting rights history to argue that women were more oppressed than enslaved people — while her daughter is carrying a mixed-race child — is peak Selina moral blindness. 'Sistas!' as the closing bid for solidarity is wonderfully awful. Character perfectly integrated.

jokes.text: Well, the Voting Rights Act was in 1965.
jokes.setup: Selina argues women were more oppressed because they got the vote 50 years after Black Americans
jokes.punchline: Mike quietly notes that the Voting Rights Act — meaning Black Americans couldn't freely vote until 1965 — deflates her entire argument with one sentence
jokes.explanation: The straight-man correction here is perfectly deployed — one quiet factual sentence that obliterates Selina's confident historical argument. Mike's accuracy arriving unexpectedly is funny in itself (he's usually wrong). The economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Mom, you are going to be the grandmother of a child that is half black. And 1/16 French Huguenot.
jokes.setup: Catherine tries to appeal to Selina's awareness of her grandchild's racial heritage
jokes.punchline: Selina's response is to flag '1/16 French Huguenot' — a completely irrelevant and obsessively specific ancestry detail that deflects the moral point
jokes.explanation: '1/16 French Huguenot' is brilliant specificity — it's the aristocratic white ancestry she wants to emphasize when the conversation turns to race. The precision (1/16!) is the joke. Pure Selina evasion through genealogical pedantry.

jokes.text: I cannot stand that term. No, I'm talking about grandmother. Okay? I am still a young woman. Yes. Really! Look at my hands. Look at my neck, okay? I am not about to be buried in a twat of my own making!
jokes.setup: Marjorie mentions nomenclature; Catherine says Selina will be a grandmother
jokes.punchline: 'I am not about to be buried in a twat of my own making!' — Selina's existential rejection of both grandmotherhood and the vagina library/crypt, condensed into one magnificent sentence
jokes.explanation: This is a contender for the episode's single best line. 'Buried in a twat of my own making' works on three levels simultaneously: (1) the literal vagina-shaped library with a crypt, (2) the metaphorical 'bed you've made' idiom, (3) Selina's vanity (refusing grandmother status). The escalation from 'look at my neck' to this declaration is perfectly structured. Every word is load-bearing. Genuine television comedy writing excellence.

jokes.text: Vice President Selina Meyer to see the president. Oh, hey, Ben. Madam Vice President. How are you? I'm good, thank you. The president is very, very busy right now. Why don't I show you your office first? Oh, sure. Yeah! Okay? Right across the street here. What? The EEOB?
jokes.setup: Selina arrives at the West Wing expecting an office inside
jokes.punchline: She's being redirected to the EEOB — the building across the street — a classic VP humiliation
jokes.explanation: This is a Season 1 flashback — the classic Veep premise in action. The humor is in how quickly Selina's expectations are managed down. Works as structural comedy even if not particularly quotable.

jokes.text: 'Nobody likes a tattletale, Marjorie.'
jokes.setup: After the explosive speech about not being buried in a twat, Selina addresses Marjorie for overhearing the slave quarters secret
jokes.punchline: 'Nobody likes a tattletale' — a kindergarten-level rebuke delivered to a grown adult about a slavery cover-up — the deflation after the explosion is perfect
jokes.explanation: The tonal whiplash after Selina's explosive 'twat of my own making' speech makes this even funnier. A children's social contract rule applied to an ethics catastrophe. Pure Selina economy.

jokes.text: Okay, I haven't spoken to my neighbors in my entire fucking life. I don't even know who they are. Oh, be glad. I've met them.
jokes.setup: Ben tells Selina her EEOB office will make them 'neighbors'
jokes.punchline: Selina says she's never spoken to her real neighbors; Ben says 'be glad, I've met them' — inverting the neighborly premise
jokes.explanation: Good two-beat joke — Selina's anti-social admission lands, then Ben's 'be glad, I've met them' flip is a quick, sharp character beat. Economy is good.

jokes.text: All right? You and I both know that Hughes would never do this if I was a male VP. We'd be out, shotgun and beers and sucking each other off like Carter and Mondale.
jokes.setup: Selina argues her EEOB assignment is sexist
jokes.punchline: Her vision of male VP camaraderie — 'shotgun and beers and sucking each other off like Carter and Mondale' — is a hilariously specific and vulgar image of male bonding politics
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character comedy. The specific real-president name-drop (Carter and Mondale) combined with the crude sexual imagery makes this perfectly Selina — political acuity expressed through vulgarity. 'Sucking each other off like Carter and Mondale' is an image that can't be unseen. One of the episode's top lines.

jokes.text: The president doesn't actually want you to do anything other than continue to be a woman, which you're doing a pretty okay job at.
jokes.setup: Ben explains to Selina why she's been assigned to the EEOB
jokes.punchline: 'Continue to be a woman, which you're doing a pretty okay job at' — a devastating non-compliment that reduces her entire VP role to gender optics
jokes.explanation: The 'pretty okay job at' qualifier on 'being a woman' is brilliantly calibrated — it's the exact level of faint praise that makes it devastating. Ben's blunt honesty about Selina's purely decorative VP role is a sharp political satire point delivered with perfect comic economy. A standout line.

jokes.text: I'm trying to cut down on exercise, okay? He'll call you if he needs you.
jokes.setup: Ben declines to accompany Selina the rest of the way to the EEOB
jokes.punchline: 'I'm trying to cut down on exercise' — mock-reasonable excuse for not walking across the street
jokes.explanation: Ben's casual refusal disguised as health advice is a good throw-away line. Not particularly crafted but delivered with the right Veep energy.

jokes.text: We are going to be in the EEOB. Oh. Yeah. No, it's good. I'm thinking we need to distance ourselves from this sort of 'one and done' administration. So, this is your decision?
jokes.setup: Selina tells Amy they're in the EEOB
jokes.punchline: Selina immediately reframes it as her own strategic decision — 'No, it's good. I'm thinking we need to distance ourselves...' Amy's 'So, this is your decision?' punctures the spin instantly
jokes.explanation: Selina's instant spin is a perfect character beat, and Amy's dry deflation of it is equally characteristic. Two-beat delivery that works cleanly.

jokes.text: I am... oh, I am a West Wing intern. Oh, congratulations. Okay. I just wanna say it's such an honor to meet you. The only reason that I voted for Hughes is because you were on the ticket. Well, I'm sure you're not alone.
jokes.setup: Young intern Jonah introduces himself to VP-elect Selina
jokes.punchline: He voted for Hughes only because of Selina; she says 'I'm sure you're not alone' — an inadvertent statement of political reality that stings
jokes.explanation: This is a flashback scene showing Jonah as a young West Wing intern — the dramatic irony of knowing where these characters end up adds rewatch value. Selina's 'I'm sure you're not alone' is both a gracious acknowledgment and a quietly devastating comment on Hughes's limitations.

jokes.text: Maybe we can pull some strings and get you assigned to the EEOB. Yeah... maybe.
jokes.setup: Selina, just told she's being sent to the EEOB, sarcastically offers to get the intern assigned there too
jokes.punchline: She's just been demoted to the EEOB herself — offering it as a 'favor' via string-pulling is darkly ironic
jokes.explanation: Quick, sharp irony — Selina 'offering' the EEOB as a perk right after being assigned there herself. Works well in the flashback context.

jokes.text: One... get the fuck away from me. Two... stay the fuck away from me. Okay? Can you do that? Yes, sir. I won't let you down. God, I love interns.
jokes.setup: Young intern Jonah enthusiastically introduces himself to a senior official, offering his help
jokes.punchline: The two tasks are: '1. Get the fuck away from me. 2. Stay the fuck away from me.' Jonah's 'I won't let you down' and the 'God, I love interns' kicker
jokes.explanation: Structurally excellent — the two-part task structure raises expectations for actual tasks, then delivers the double-barreled rejection. Jonah's earnest 'I won't let you down' is perfectly in character (even as an intern he has no self-awareness), and 'God, I love interns' as the kicker reframes it as general contempt rather than personal. One of the episode's best constructed jokes.

jokes.text: Pass mandatory sentencing laws for vagrancy. Then these unfortunates will finally have a home... My prisons.
jokes.setup: A congressman pitches a solution to homelessness
jokes.punchline: 'My prisons' — he owns private prisons; his 'solution' to homelessness is imprisoning the homeless for his own profit
jokes.explanation: 'My prisons' is a perfectly delivered punchline — the pause implied in the construction before the reveal is well-structured. The private prison corruption joke is on-brand for Veep's political satire.

jokes.text: Look, I hate the homeless as much as any librarian. That's why God created subzero winters and the third rail.
jokes.setup: The congressman continues his pitch on homeless policy
jokes.punchline: 'God created subzero winters and the third rail' as the natural homeless solution — casual advocacy for death-by-exposure and electrocution
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of 'I hate the homeless as much as any librarian' (normal person comparison) with the death-wish punchline is a good dark escalation. The specificity of 'third rail' alongside 'subzero winters' is vivid.

jokes.text: chances of me pushing for any tax incentives for these private hoosegows is about as likely as what, Will? As me walking out of a bar with less than 10 types of semen in my hair.
jokes.setup: The congressman asks Will to complete his simile about the likelihood of supporting private prison tax incentives
jokes.punchline: Will's answer — 'me walking out of a bar with less than 10 types of semen in my hair' — is shockingly specific and graphic, delivered straight
jokes.explanation: The 'fill in the simile' structure creates expectation, then Will's self-deprecating sexual confession is wonderfully specific and extreme. '10 types' is the exact right specificity — not 'some' or 'a lot' but a precise count. Dark and surprising.

jokes.text: I can't believe you're actually showing that camel snatch you call a face in DC. You're about as welcome here as Jerry Sandusky at an open call for 'Oliver.'
jokes.setup: Jonah appears uninvited at Furlong's office
jokes.punchline: 'Camel snatch you call a face' + the Sandusky/Oliver comparison — two-tier Furlong insult volley
jokes.explanation: Furlong's insults are one of the show's most reliable comedy engines. 'Camel snatch you call a face' is vivid and specific. The Sandusky/Oliver pairing works because it's specific (open call for a children's musical) — though the Sandusky reference has aged somewhat. High character integration: nobody but Furlong says things like this.

jokes.text: Here, you want a dried apricot? They're Turkish. Anything I can do to annoy the Armenians.
jokes.setup: Furlong offers Jonah a snack mid-tirade
jokes.punchline: The apricots are Turkish — specifically chosen to annoy the Armenians — a casual genocide-adjacent political maneuver delivered while offering a snack
jokes.explanation: This is a masterclass in Veep dark comedy — using Turkish apricots as a political statement about the Armenian genocide, delivered while snacking, mid-insult. The casualness is everything. The specificity (apricots as the geopolitical weapon of choice) is original and hilarious. Strong candidate for episode highlight.

jokes.text: Oh, hey, Jonah, can we get lunch sometime? I wanna talk about you being the legal guardian to my baby if anything happens to me and Mary, okay? Yeah, sure.
jokes.setup: After demolishing Jonah with insults, Furlong suddenly turns casual and friendly
jokes.punchline: He wants Jonah as his baby's legal guardian — the most vulnerable parental role offered to the person he just called a 'camel snatch'
jokes.explanation: The tonal whiplash from vicious insult to 'can we get lunch' to 'be my baby's guardian' is extraordinary. Furlong asking Jonah — the creature he just degraded — to be legal guardian reveals a genuine human connection beneath the contempt. This subversion is one of the episode's most inventive moments.

jokes.text: Congressman on Brokeback Mountain: 'It's like that fagela cowboy movie. "I can't quit you."' / Jonah: 'Yes, thank you, sir. Butch and Sundance.'
jokes.setup: The congressman expresses his complicated feelings about Jonah by referencing Brokeback Mountain
jokes.punchline: Jonah — trying to show he knows the reference — calls it 'Butch and Sundance,' completely wrong film, further revealing his stupidity
jokes.explanation: Two layers: the congressman's offensive-affectionate Brokeback reference, and then Jonah's confidently wrong correction ('Butch and Sundance'). Both characters are perfectly themselves. The wrong film name is a great Jonah stupidity joke.

jokes.text: No! From now on, I'm only buying senators and presidents.
jokes.setup: Furlong advises Jonah not to pursue individual representatives and congressmen
jokes.punchline: Framing political corruption as a simple market segmentation decision — 'I'm only buying senators and presidents' — with complete casualness
jokes.explanation: The frank reduction of political influence-buying to a retail versus wholesale choice is sharp satire. 'Buying senators and presidents' delivered as obvious business logic is Veep political commentary at its most efficient.

jokes.text: We'll always have the shutdown, won't we? Huge miscalculation.
jokes.setup: A Casablanca-style sentimental 'we'll always have...' moment between Furlong and Jonah
jokes.punchline: What they 'always have' is a government shutdown — Jonah's proudest achievement was actually a catastrophic political failure
jokes.explanation: The Casablanca riff is perfectly deployed — 'We'll always have the shutdown' sets up a moment of genuine connection, then 'Huge miscalculation' immediately frames it as an objective failure. Economy is exceptional: three words of punchline do all the work.

jokes.text: Mayor Thompson, I hope you'll support me in November in the Senate contest. Absolutely, Congresswoman Meyers. Thank you. No, op, op, op. It's Meyer. There's no 'S' at the end... Oh, Jewish.
jokes.setup: Selina corrects Mayor Thompson's mispronunciation of her name
jokes.punchline: Thompson's response to being told there's no 'S' is 'Oh, Jewish' — a non-sequitur ethnic assumption that makes everything worse
jokes.explanation: The leap from 'no S at the end' to 'Oh, Jewish' is a brilliantly absurd piece of local politician logic. The non-sequitur is specific enough to feel real — the kind of thing a certain type of small-town politician actually says. Good cringe comedy.

jokes.text: Oh, what a great county this is. I just love... everybody here is so well-behaved and polite. Thank you! What joy! We gotta go. It is like Selinamania, right?
jokes.setup: Selina is campaigning at what appears to be a small local event
jokes.punchline: She describes the crowd as 'well-behaved and polite' as if they're toddlers, then calls it 'Selinamania' — grandiose self-branding for a modest turnout
jokes.explanation: 'Selinamania' is a good bit of self-aggrandizing that reveals her political delusion. 'Well-behaved and polite' as her highest compliment for voters is appropriately condescending.

jokes.text: Selina, we are fucked two ways which till Tuesday. What are you talking about? We've completely spent all of our TV money. Well, who's in charge of that? Technically, I am, but when you buy these ads...
jokes.setup: Mike informs Selina of a campaign crisis
jokes.punchline: When asked who's in charge of TV money, Mike says 'Technically, I am' — then immediately begins explaining why it's not his fault
jokes.explanation: 'Technically, I am' is a good Mike beat — the immediate defensive qualifier is in character. 'Fucked two ways which till Tuesday' is a mangled idiom that's nicely specific but not as sharp as some others.

jokes.text: Hey, Andrew, we have gotta fire that Mike guy. He's the biggest goddamn idiot I've ever... Selina! Hey! Uh, boy, I got... Can you believe I put a pen in my pocket without a cap? And Sally was helping me get the stain out.
jokes.setup: Selina is mid-sentence firing Mike when Andrew interrupts with an alibi about a pen
jokes.punchline: The pen stain on his shirt is clearly cover for something else with Sally (the donor); the innocent explanation is obviously false
jokes.explanation: Classic Andrew cover-story comedy — his 'pen' excuse is transparently false and sets up his leverage over Sally. The mid-sentence interruption cutting off Selina's Mike-firing speech adds good timing.

jokes.text: Yeah, you know, I wish your husband was here because I would love for him to hear about the story of you and Andrew on the bus with the pen. Let me get my checkbook.
jokes.setup: Sally says she's already donated to other candidates
jokes.punchline: Selina immediately weaponizes the pen story (Andrew's affair) as blackmail leverage to extract a donation
jokes.explanation: This is a great comedy beat — Selina uses implied infidelity blackmail to fundraise, completely casually. The callback to the pen story is well-earned, and 'Let me get my checkbook' as the instant capitulation is perfectly paced. Selina's ruthlessness as a comedy engine is peak form here.

jokes.text: why don't you put your money where your mouth was... is... And add another zero? That should be no problem. Oh, goody!
jokes.setup: Sally offers $25,000
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'put your money where your mouth was... is...' stumble followed by the ask for another zero — the self-correction mid-extortion and her 'Oh, goody!' response
jokes.explanation: 'Oh, goody!' is a good Selina vocal, the fumbled idiom mid-extortion is character-true. Functional comedy but not as precise as the setup beat.

jokes.text: How about an interview with the soldier that you pulled from the tank? That's an excellent idea. Yeah, thank you. But best not to contact him for personal reasons, or, really, anyone who saw it. I don't wanna retrigger.
jokes.setup: Amy suggests interviewing the soldier Chung claims to have saved from a burning tank
jokes.punchline: 'Best not to contact him for personal reasons, or really anyone who saw it' — implying the story is fabricated or embellished
jokes.explanation: The escalating list of who not to contact — the soldier, 'or really anyone who saw it' — cleanly signals the war story is false. 'I don't wanna retrigger' as cover is a nice contemporary euphemism deployed cynically.

jokes.text: Ready for a change, ready for Chung. Ready for a 'chunge.' First 'chunge,' we're gonna fix that slogan. Really? I'm kidding. It's great.
jokes.setup: Chung's campaign slogan is 'Ready for a change, ready for Chung'
jokes.punchline: Amy fuses them into 'chunge,' then when Chung says they'll fix it, she backtracks — 'I'm kidding. It's great.'
jokes.explanation: 'Chunge' is a genuinely funny coinage. Amy's immediate retreat ('I'm kidding. It's great.') when challenged is good character work — she's already calculating whether this job is worth it.

jokes.text: Give us a little touch of the feminine. You and I could split those duties. Yeah, I think I, uh... I've been waiting a long time for us to get together. There it is. There we go. Back at full strength!
jokes.setup: Chung and Dan are trying to recruit Amy to the team
jokes.punchline: Dan's 'I've been waiting a long time for us to get together' — openly hitting on Amy in a professional context; 'There it is' as the group acknowledgment
jokes.explanation: Dan's sexual innuendo in a professional setting is a reliable but not exceptional beat. 'There it is' as the group recognition of the hit is nicely calibrated. Functional cringe comedy.

jokes.text: Oh, Amy's out. No! Thanks for coming in, Ame. No! We're not gonna validate your parking. You don't mind that, do you?
jokes.setup: Amy says she needs to talk to Selina first
jokes.punchline: They immediately declare her 'out' and deny her parking validation — the dismissiveness of the parking line as the petty final word
jokes.explanation: The parking validation as the petty capper to Amy being fired from a job she hasn't taken is a decent bit of business. Functional but not sharp.

jokes.text: Okay, so Lu wants to meet in Hong Kong to talk Brazil. We'll leave Friday. That's so funny because the lady who does my Brazilian is from Hong Kong. Oh! It's Kismit. I know. It really is, isn't it? No, her name is Kismit. They make her use Linda, but...
jokes.setup: Jaffar mentions going to Hong Kong to discuss Brazil (the country)
jokes.punchline: Selina hears 'Brazilian' (the wax) and makes a connection; then the 'Kismit/kismet' mix-up compounds the misunderstanding
jokes.explanation: Three-layer joke: Brazil/Brazilian (geopolitical to grooming), Kismit/kismet (name/concept confusion), and the detail that they make her use Linda — all delivered at speed. Characteristically Selina in the way the personal always supersedes the political. Well-constructed wordplay.

jokes.text: You know, my favorite cousin Hamid went here. He partied his balls off. Oh, my God, we should've invited him to come to the thing today. Oh, he's not exactly on the yes-fly list.
jokes.setup: Jaffar mentions his cousin Hamid went to Yale
jokes.punchline: 'He's not exactly on the yes-fly list' — a casual reference to his cousin being on a no-fly list, delivered conversationally
jokes.explanation: 'Yes-fly list' as the friendly inversion of no-fly list is a great coinage. The casualness of Jaffar mentioning his cousin's terrorism-adjacent status while discussing a Yale event is perfectly dark. Character-specific: Jaffar's aristocratic nonchalance about such things is a consistent character note.

jokes.text: Mom, I'm so glad my doctor cleared me to come today. But you gotta be careful with that McLintock cervix of yours. Oh, it's incompetent cervix, ma'am. That's what I said, Marjorie. Zing! You got me, ma'am.
jokes.setup: Catherine mentions her medical clearance for the event
jokes.punchline: Selina calls it the 'McLintock cervix' (her ex-husband's surname applied as a possessive to a medical condition); when corrected, she insists 'that's what I said'; Marjorie's flat 'Zing! You got me' is the kicker
jokes.explanation: 'McLintock cervix' is a wonderfully specific malaprops that somehow works as a put-down. Marjorie's deadpan 'Zing! You got me' as robotic irony is a great character beat — she's playing along with Selina's games without genuine feeling.

jokes.text: My God, looks like Queen Kong's cooter. That's what we were going for! ... That ain't the first time I spent 20 million on a piece of pussy. Oh, good on you, Quartie.
jokes.setup: An older male character (Quartie) comments on the vagina-shaped library design
jokes.punchline: 'That ain't the first time I spent 20 million on a piece of pussy' — a shocking admission of past sexual expenditure delivered as a compliment to the architecture
jokes.explanation: The line lands hard because it's simultaneously revolting and oddly proud — a man using his sexual history as architectural credentials. 'Queen Kong's cooter' is also a strong image coinage. Selina's 'good on you, Quartie' as breezy acceptance is perfect.

jokes.text: 'The chicken, right?' — Selina confuses a decision about who runs the library with a previous chicken discussion
jokes.setup: Gary tells Selina she needs to make a decision; she confidently guesses 'the chicken'
jokes.punchline: The entirely wrong answer — she has confused a personnel/library decision with a meal preference discussion — reveals how disengaged Selina is from her own operation
jokes.explanation: Good character beat. Selina's confident wrong answer about 'the chicken' is a funny encapsulation of her selective attention.

jokes.text: Well, I've got a professor of gender studies suing for tenure. You can imagine what that's about.
jokes.setup: Jim mentions his day's problems
jokes.punchline: 'You can imagine what that's about' — a dismissive aside implying gender studies tenure suits are a known, tiresome category
jokes.explanation: A quick throwaway that works as a character beat for Jim. The knowing 'you can imagine what that's about' gesture is functional but not particularly sharp.

jokes.text: If it were up to me, we'd be putting up drywall on your vagina building today.
jokes.setup: Jim apologizes for having to delay the groundbreaking
jokes.punchline: His expression of enthusiasm for the project — 'putting up drywall on your vagina building' — using the staff nickname for the library unselfconsciously
jokes.explanation: A Yale president casually saying 'vagina building' to the former president is a good social incongruity joke. The callback value adds a layer.

jokes.text: with the lawsuit and with one of our adjuncts in the Hillel function with the sombrero... I don't know what that is.
jokes.setup: Jim lists the reasons Yale can't proceed with the library
jokes.punchline: An adjunct professor at a Jewish event wearing a sombrero — an entirely unexplained, maximally incongruous image — gets Selina's flat 'I don't know what that is'
jokes.explanation: 'Adjunct at the Hillel function with the sombrero' is a purely imagistic absurdist joke — the specificity and incongruity are everything. Selina's 'I don't know what that is' refusal to engage is the perfect response. Very Veep in its compressed worldbuilding.

jokes.text: After all, this is Yale, not Brown. I got waitlisted there. Oh, oh, ow, ow, ow!
jokes.setup: Jim says Yale doesn't want attention on its historical slavery associations
jokes.punchline: Selina processes the Brown University aside and reacts physically — she was waitlisted at Brown, which is apparently still a wound
jokes.explanation: The Brown waitlist joke is excellent — it reveals that a former president is still wounded by a college rejection, which is both pathetically human and specifically Selina. The physical 'ow ow ow' reaction to an old wound is great physical comedy conveyed through text. Character perfectly integrated.

jokes.text: The KKK... that's the Kool Kweers of Kolor... is having a 'pee-in' in front of Skull and Bones.
jokes.setup: Jim mentions additional campus complications
jokes.punchline: He clarifies the KKK initialism as 'Kool Kweers of Kolor' — an earnest Yale-speak organization name that repurposes the initials — staging a 'pee-in'
jokes.explanation: Using KKK as an acronym for a queer-of-color activist group is a sharp piece of institutional absurdism. The 'pee-in' as protest form (riffing on 'sit-in') adds an additional layer of silliness. Very Yale.

jokes.text: Yale pulled out without even coming on my tits? Things sure have changed since I went to college.
jokes.setup: Yale has pulled out of the library deal at the last minute
jokes.punchline: Selina reframes 'pulling out' in sexual terms — the metaphor is both vulgar and characteristically Selina in its shamelessness
jokes.explanation: A strong Selina line — the sexual metaphor for institutional betrayal, delivered with practiced casualness. 'Things sure have changed since I went to college' is the perfect nostalgic kicker. High quotability. Captures Selina's vulgar pragmatism.

jokes.text: Doctor says name; Selina mishears: 'Sengupta.' / 'Gundupta.' / 'I'm gonna get you a cookie.'
jokes.setup: Selina asks the doctor's name; doctor says 'Sengupta'
jokes.punchline: Selina immediately mishears/manbles it as 'Gundupta' — a completely wrong name — and the doctor's 'I'm gonna get you a cookie' response implies she's said something childlike or offensive
jokes.explanation: Good cringe beat. 'Gundupta' is a comedic mispronunciation. The doctor's 'I'm gonna get you a cookie' response perfectly handles the insult with patronizing grace.

jokes.text: Have you decided on a name? No. What's your name? Sandra. Yeah, not that.
jokes.setup: Doctor asks about the baby's name
jokes.punchline: Catherine asks the doctor's name as if considering it, then immediately rejects it — 'Yeah, not that'
jokes.explanation: 'Yeah, not that' is a wonderfully economical dismissal. The setup (asking the doctor's name sincerely) creates just enough space for the rejection to land. Good economy.

jokes.text: Hi, I'm Gary. Hi. I ground these myself. Okay. The hospital ones are so big.
jokes.setup: Gary introduces himself to hospital staff and offers hand-ground ice chips
jokes.punchline: 'I ground these myself' + 'The hospital ones are so big' — Gary's entire purpose in life being personally hand-ground ice chips for Selina's family
jokes.explanation: Gary grinding his own ice chips for a labor room is peak Gary — his devoted servitude taken to its logical, slightly disturbing extreme. 'The hospital ones are so big' as a complaint is genuinely funny because it reveals he has opinions about ice chip size. Very high character integration.

jokes.text: Okay, Doctor... Sengupta. Gundupta. I'm gonna get you a cookie.
jokes.setup: Gary tries to address the doctor by name
jokes.punchline: 'Gundupta' — Gary's mispronunciation of 'Sengupta', then Gary offering to get the doctor a cookie as his anxiety management
jokes.explanation: The mispronunciation is a bit lazy — it's Gary being bad at an unfamiliar name. The cookie offer is a better beat. Functional but below the episode's best work.

jokes.text: Can you write down your number in case we have any questions about it? Okay. You can give it to my husband, Andrew. Hi.
jokes.setup: Catherine asks nurse Sandra for her number 'in case of questions'
jokes.punchline: She specifically asks Sandra to give it to Andrew — implying she knows Andrew will pursue Sandra. The 'Hi' from Andrew is the guilty, flirtatious confirmation.
jokes.explanation: The setup and punchline play perfectly across multiple characters — Catherine's knowing facilitation of her husband's infidelity, followed by Andrew's simple 'Hi.' as both greeting and self-incrimination. Dark and efficient.

jokes.text: I think I wanna run for Congress. Huh. Oh, some black shit is coming out of it. Good... yeah. Loud. It's so loud. I've never heard anything quite like... ooh. Ugh, it's loud.
jokes.setup: Someone announces they want to run for Congress in the delivery room
jokes.punchline: The response is entirely about the newborn's meconium and noise level — politics completely displaced by biological reality
jokes.explanation: The political announcement completely upstaged by infant bodily functions is a good structural joke. The delivery room as the worst possible venue for a campaign announcement, then the announcement being ignored — character-specific and well-timed.

jokes.text: Well, if we're judging by sex organs, which apparently we're not, it's a boy.
jokes.setup: Selina asks what the baby is — boy or girl
jokes.punchline: Marjorie's flat acknowledgment of the trans/gender-fluid social convention as a caveat before announcing 'it's a boy' — delivered completely without editorial
jokes.explanation: This is classic Marjorie — delivering a progressive social disclaimer with complete robotic neutrality. 'Which apparently we're not' is the key phrase: she's noting the social norm without endorsing or mocking it. The deadpan is perfect. High character integration.

jokes.text: Selina comments on the baby's loudness: 'Loud. It's so loud. I've never heard anything quite like... ooh. Ugh, it's loud.'
jokes.setup: The newborn is crying
jokes.punchline: Selina's repeated 'it's loud' complaints about her newborn grandchild, delivered with the affect of someone reviewing a bad restaurant
jokes.explanation: Serviceable character comedy — Selina reviewing the baby's noise level. Not as sharp as the surrounding jokes but consistent with her character.

jokes.text: I'll just give him a boop-boop, pat-pat. Monnie. Oh! Grandma! Yeah. Congratulations. Okay, when did you come back into the picture? What do you mean? Nope. Mm-mm.
jokes.setup: Selina reluctantly holds the baby, then an unexpected relative appears
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'Okay, when did you come back into the picture?' + 'Nope. Mm-mm.' — someone she had written off has reappeared at the birth
jokes.explanation: The confused family dynamics and Selina's flat 'Nope. Mm-mm.' to an unwanted person is a functional beat but requires context to fully land. Moderately amusing.

jokes.text: Selina arrives late: 'You went ahead without me.' / 'The baby has my elbows.'
jokes.setup: Selina arrives after the birth, acting put-upon that they proceeded without her
jokes.punchline: Catherine tries to include Selina by noting the baby has her elbows — but in context (Selina arrived late to the birth), it's a darkly inadequate consolation
jokes.explanation: Good timing joke — 'you went ahead without me' as a complaint about a birth is funny, and 'the baby has my elbows' as a peace offering is gently absurd.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, I can't believe it! You're Monica from 'Monica.' It is a dream of mine to cook with you someday. It's my dream, too. Aw, well, we should make that happen. I'll get your number. Absolutely, we should make that happen. Go, just go.
jokes.setup: A celebrity TV chef has appeared in the delivery room
jokes.punchline: Catherine, in a hospital bed having just given birth, is starstruck by a cooking show host. Selina dismisses the whole exchange — 'Go, just go.'
jokes.explanation: The delivery room celebrity encounter is absurd and Selina's dismissiveness is good. 'You're Monica from Monica' is a funny non-name brand name. A bit flat compared to surrounding material.

jokes.text: Okay, so what are we calling him? Aluminum? Tenafly? Ribbon? We haven't landed on a name yet. I don't mind Tenafly. You know, what about Richard? That would be kind of cute 'cause then we could call him Little Richard. Love! I was actually named after my godmother, Richardina. Everybody just called her Regina.
jokes.setup: Discussion of the baby's name
jokes.punchline: Selina suggests 'Aluminum, Tenafly, Ribbon' as options; Richard enthusiastically endorses 'Little Richard'; then reveals he was named after 'Richardina' who was called 'Regina'
jokes.explanation: Multiple beats: the absurd name suggestions, 'Little Richard' enthusiasm, and Richard's meandering godmother story arriving at 'Richardina... everyone called her Regina' — the last bit is pure Richard: long, earnest, ultimately pointless. Good character work.

jokes.text: Baby name suggestions: 'Aluminum? Tenafly? Ribbon?' / 'I don't mind Tenafly.'
jokes.setup: The group discusses baby names
jokes.punchline: Selina's suggestions are completely abstract nouns and proper nouns — 'Aluminum,' 'Tenafly,' 'Ribbon' — and Andrew's comfortable 'I don't mind Tenafly' suggests these are not as insane to him as they are to us
jokes.explanation: Absurdist name comedy. The escalation of 'Aluminum' → 'Tenafly' → 'Ribbon' is inspired, and Andrew's genuine consideration of 'Tenafly' is perfect. These are the baby names of a person who has been in politics too long.

jokes.text: Richard's name origin: 'I was actually named after my godmother, Richardina. Everybody just called her Regina. Oh, boy. You know what, actually, I probably wasn't named after her.'
jokes.setup: The group discusses naming the baby Richard; Richard shares his name's origin
jokes.punchline: Richard traces his name to Richardina → Regina → then immediately admits he probably wasn't named after her at all — his own origin story collapses in real time
jokes.explanation: A masterful Richard Splett joke. He starts a confident narrative (Richardina), realizes the logic doesn't hold (everyone called her Regina), and then withdraws the entire story mid-sentence. Richard's earnest self-undermining is at its finest here. 'Oh, boy' as his self-correction is perfect.

jokes.text: So, in the Quran, it is recommended that upon the birth of a child, a father should slaughter two goats. However, out of respect for your veganism, I got you the wipe warmer instead.
jokes.setup: Jaffar presents a baby gift with religious context
jokes.punchline: The Islamic goat slaughter tradition vs. vegan wipe warmer substitution — a perfect cultural accommodation joke
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best constructed jokes. The structure is impeccable: serious religious citation, specific accommodation ('respect for your veganism'), absurd equivalent gift. Jaffar's aristocratic formality makes the wipe warmer substitution funnier. The joke manages to be about cultural difference, veganism, and parenting anxiety simultaneously without straining. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: Ambassador Jaffar, if I could nibble your ear for a moment about a sand opportunity I'm currently involved with. I'm all set with sand. But this is beach sand. He doesn't want sand.
jokes.setup: Andrew tries to pitch Jaffar a business deal at a maternity ward
jokes.punchline: His mysterious 'sand opportunity' is met with 'I'm all set with sand'; Andrew insists 'but this is beach sand'; Selina flatly: 'He doesn't want sand.'
jokes.explanation: Andrew pitching sand in a maternity ward to a Gulf state ambassador is wonderfully specific and absurd. 'But this is beach sand' as the differentiating value proposition is exactly the kind of desperate specificity that makes Andrew pathetic and funny. Selina's 'He doesn't want sand' is the perfect deadpan close.

jokes.text: I would rather never have a presidential library than to have one that is built on the backs of dead slaves. Shame on Yale. And shame on Amy Brookheimer. Yes. Oh, and this is my lesbian daughter's Native American lesbian life partner, Marjorie.
jokes.setup: Selina delivers a moving press statement about refusing to build on slave quarters
jokes.punchline: The gravitas is immediately undercut by 'And shame on Amy Brookheimer' — a personal grudge smuggled into a civil rights statement — then the absurdly over-specified introduction of Marjorie
jokes.explanation: Triple-level joke: (1) Selina's principled slave-quarters stand is genuinely calculated, (2) 'shame on Amy Brookheimer' is the personal grudge that reveals the calculation, (3) 'my lesbian daughter's Native American lesbian life partner, Marjorie' — the identity stacking for political optics is a perfectly Selina move. Character integration is excellent.

jokes.text: Selina's speech: 'as the proud grand... Mother.'
jokes.setup: Selina begins to speak about being the proud grandmother
jokes.punchline: She stops herself and says 'Mother' — still refusing to say 'grandmother' even in a public speech
jokes.explanation: Solid callback payoff on the grandmother running gag. The self-correction in the middle of a public statement is good.

jokes.text: 'And this is my lesbian daughter's Native American lesbian life partner, Marjorie.'
jokes.setup: Selina introduces Marjorie in her press statement about the library
jokes.punchline: The over-specification — 'my lesbian daughter's Native American lesbian life partner' — stacks identity labels in a way that reveals Selina's performative wokeness and complete tone-deafness simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Good satire of performative identity politics. The doubling of 'lesbian' (once for Catherine, once for Marjorie) is funny, and 'Native American' feels like Selina reaching for the maximum diversity points available.

jokes.text: Both my daughter and my mother have struggled greatly with very serious mental health problems. What? Don't worry, it runs in my family, too.
jokes.setup: Selina makes a public statement about mental health to deflect the Whispering Sands story
jokes.punchline: She throws her daughter and mother under the bus to deflect attention from herself, then adds 'it runs in my family too' — inadvertently (or cleverly) including herself
jokes.explanation: The callback resolves the spa running gag — Selina uses mental health deflection, outing Catherine and her own mother, to avoid outing herself. 'Don't worry, it runs in my family too' is the dark kicker that applies to all three generations.

jokes.text: Catherine: 'What?' / Selina: 'Don't worry, it runs in my family, too.'
jokes.setup: Catherine is horrified that Selina just claimed she has mental health problems
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'Don't worry, it runs in my family' — which (a) is meant to be reassuring (b) is also true and (c) implies Selina is also confirming her own mental health history while trying to deny it
jokes.explanation: Brilliant follow-up to the previous joke. The 'it runs in my family' reassurance inadvertently confirms Selina's own mental health issues while also being the most tone-deaf possible comfort. The triple meaning (reassurance / confirmation / family history) is tightly constructed.

jokes.text: I dreamt that I removed Leon West's balls with an ice cream scoop and I think I actually came. Is everything always ice cream with you?
jokes.setup: Selina tells Amy about a dream involving Leon West
jokes.punchline: 'I removed Leon West's balls with an ice cream scoop and I think I actually came' — Amy's response: 'Is everything always ice cream with you?'
jokes.explanation: A standout moment. The castration fantasy with the ice cream scoop is vivid and specific (the household utensil choice is perfect), and the orgasm admission takes it a step further. Amy's callback to ice cream ('Is everything always ice cream with you?') references prior Selina ice cream moments (if any) while being a perfect comic deflation. The specificity of 'ice cream scoop' over any other tool is the key creative decision here. High quotability.

jokes.text: Why? Why would you Kurt Cobain your own library?
jokes.setup: Amy discovers Selina leaked the slave quarters story to Leon West
jokes.punchline: 'Kurt Cobain your own library' — using the musician's suicide as a verb for self-sabotage
jokes.explanation: Extremely economical and original. 'Kurt Cobain' as a verb for self-sabotage is a genuinely fresh coinage — the specificity of the celebrity reference, the dark implication, and the institutional object ('your own library') combine for maximum compression. Very Veep in its dark celebrity wordplay. High quotability.

jokes.text: Because, girlie, only former presidents have libraries. And I'm running for president.
jokes.setup: Amy can't understand why Selina would destroy her own library
jokes.punchline: The reveal: Selina sabotaged the library because having a library as a former VP signals she's finished — and she's running for president again
jokes.explanation: The episode's structural climax — the entire library plot was Selina's long game to re-establish her presidential credentials by sacrificing it on the altar of principle. The 'girlie' is perfectly patronizing. The logic is characteristically Selina: cynical, brilliant, self-serving, and ultimately correct. Perfect structural payoff for the entire episode. The rewatch value is enormous because every prior library scene is retroactively funnier.

jokes.text: The band is getting back together again! Who cc'd Mike? I get it, I'm Ringo. No, Amy's Ringo. You're Mark David Chapman's bullet.
jokes.setup: The team reassembles around Selina's presidential run announcement
jokes.punchline: Mike hopes to be Ringo (the least of the Beatles); Selina corrects — Amy's Ringo, Mike is 'Mark David Chapman's bullet' — i.e., the thing that killed the greatest thing
jokes.explanation: Possibly the episode's single best line. The Beatles hierarchy joke is set up cleanly, Ringo's consolation status is earned, then 'Mark David Chapman's bullet' is an extraordinary leap — it doesn't just insult Mike, it categorizes him as the agent of destruction of something great. The specificity (not just 'a bullet' but 'Mark David Chapman's bullet') is perfect. Highest quotability in the episode. A genuine Veep-hall-of-fame insult.

jokes.text: Tibet! Tibet! It's all Tibet! We are gonna ride that Dalai Lama like Mrs. Lama on book club night.
jokes.setup: The team discusses how Selina can win the presidency
jokes.punchline: 'We are gonna ride that Dalai Lama like Mrs. Lama on book club night' — sexualizing the Dalai Lama as a political asset
jokes.explanation: Dan using his signature crude political metaphors — this one being a sexual image involving the Dalai Lama's wife — is perfectly in character. 'Mrs. Lama on book club night' is the precise detail that makes it funny: the domestic specificity of 'book club night' against the sacred religious figure.

jokes.text: And what about Montez? She's a Mexican who stole your job. She did, in fact, steal my job. Her numbers are under agua.
jokes.setup: The team discusses the current president, Montez
jokes.punchline: 'She's a Mexican who stole your job' — a deliberately offensive framing; Selina's straight 'She did, in fact, steal my job'; 'Her numbers are under agua' — using Spanish to describe her underwater poll numbers
jokes.explanation: 'Under agua' is a lovely double-meaning play — 'underwater' in polls but also the Spanish word, which is simultaneously patronizing and clever given the context. Selina's straight endorsement of the 'Mexican who stole your job' framing is dark and character-perfect.

jokes.text: To Team Meyer. Team Meyer! Second time's the charm. It's actually fourth. Fourth. Fourth time's the charm. Yeah, that's fourth, ma'am.
jokes.setup: Selina toasts 'second time's the charm'
jokes.punchline: Multiple people correct her that it's actually her fourth run — the repetition of correction and her 'Fourth time's the charm' recovery
jokes.explanation: The corrections accumulating — multiple people confirming 'fourth' — is a good escalation beat. Selina's casual recovery ('Fourth time's the charm') is characteristic. Works well as a team moment.

jokes.text: Beats working for Jonah. Yeah.
jokes.setup: Richard is asked what he thinks about rejoining Team Selina
jokes.punchline: 'Beats working for Jonah' — the damning-with-faint-praise summary of Selina's appeal as an employer
jokes.explanation: Brilliant economy — three words that simultaneously praise Selina and condemn her by placing Jonah as the only lower bar. Pure Richard sincerity that inadvertently damns his new employer.

jokes.text: There's just one last thing you need to do. Mm, I'm not gonna do that. Selina, you have to do it. Mm-mm.
jokes.setup: Amy tells Selina there's one last thing she needs to do
jokes.punchline: Selina refuses before she even hears what it is — the thing (presumably breaking up with Jaffar) is so known between them that no explanation is needed
jokes.explanation: The unspoken understanding between Selina and Amy — the fact that Selina knows exactly what's required and refuses before hearing it — is efficient comic shorthand. Works as a bridge to the Jaffar scene.

jokes.text: You know, there's a restaurant in Kowloon that has a clay-baked chicken dish that you have to order 24 hours in advance. Yeah, Jaffar, I can't. I understand. 24 hours seems like a lot of hoopla for chicken, doesn't it?
jokes.setup: Selina is clearly about to break up with Jaffar; he talks about a restaurant
jokes.punchline: Jaffar completely understands the breakup subtext but deflects to the chicken — 'I understand. 24 hours seems like a lot of hoopla for chicken' is his way of making it less painful through absurd displacement
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most sophisticated comedy moments. Jaffar's chicken story is a displacement activity for an impending breakup — he knows what's coming. '24 hours seems like a lot of hoopla for chicken' as his way of processing abandonment is both funny and quietly devastating. The line works as comedy and as character illumination simultaneously. Jaffar's dignity in the face of rejection is the subtext that makes the chicken funnier.

jokes.text: Muslim baggage? Jaffar, you are so sophisticated... And your grasp of geopolitics is just... And those are assets anywhere in the world except... In America? In the United States, yeah. And most of Europe, except Germany, you know, 'cause they overcorrect.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to explain why being with Jaffar is politically inconvenient
jokes.punchline: The escalating hedges — 'except' America, then 'and most of Europe,' then 'except Germany, you know, cause they overcorrect' — Germany's over-correction on Muslim acceptance as the blackly comic specific exception
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Veep political comedy. The geographic exception list is perfectly structured: America (expected), most of Europe (broader), but Germany gets a carve-out because 'they overcorrect' — a brilliant historical reference to Germany's post-WWII guilt overcorrection. The escalation from personal to continental to national is perfect. One of the episode's most politically sophisticated jokes.

jokes.text: Jaffar takes the ring back. Visual beat: Selina silently accepts this.
jokes.setup: Selina has just explained she's dumping Jaffar for being Muslim; he says 'You should take this back' about the ring
jokes.punchline: 'Yes, I want it back' — Jaffar coldly reclaims the blood diamond ring; the long silence before and after is the comedy of Selina having nothing left to say
jokes.explanation: Strong callback and reaction beat. The blood diamond ring — originally introduced as a dark joke about the arms trade — is now being reclaimed in the context of Selina's racism. The silence is the joke. Jaffar's dignity throughout this scene makes Selina's behavior funnier and worse.

jokes.text: How are the girls and Big and Little Richard doing? How are your kids, Ben? Ooh, touché.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Ben about her family
jokes.punchline: Ben's 'How are your kids?' counter — implying Selina is an absent mother — then 'touché' as acknowledgment that both have complicated family relationships
jokes.explanation: The 'Big and Little Richard' nickname for the baby is a good carry from the naming scene. Ben's counter lands cleanly; 'touché' as mutual acknowledgment of bad parenting is a nice beat.

jokes.text: I was here in 2012, 'Steve Bing'd' a couple stewardesses. Will you stop being exactly like yourself? You're fucking ruining this.
jokes.setup: Ben is having a sentimental moment about the Iowa dinner
jokes.punchline: Dan's bragging about Steve Bing-ing stewardesses in 2012; Ben's 'Will you stop being exactly like yourself?' — a perfectly constructed complaint
jokes.explanation: 'Will you stop being exactly like yourself?' is an exceptional insult because it's simultaneously a complaint about a specific behavior and a philosophical indictment of someone's entire personhood. 'Steve Bing'd' as a verb for a specific type of celebrity-adjacent sexual encounter is good dark wordplay.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, I'm pregnant and it's yours. Ladies and gentlemen... Fuck!
jokes.setup: Amy and Dan are having a private conversation backstage; Selina begins her speech introduction
jokes.punchline: Amy drops the pregnancy bomb right as the public announcement begins. Dan's 'Fuck!' as Selina is introduced is the perfect cut
jokes.explanation: The timing is structurally perfect — the pregnancy reveal lands at the exact moment of maximum public contrast (Selina's triumphant introduction). The cut from 'I'm pregnant and it's yours' to 'Ladies and gentlemen...' to 'Fuck!' is masterful TV comedy editing implied by the transcript structure. One of the episode's most structurally impressive moments.

jokes.text: There's been a lot of speculation about me running for president. Again.
jokes.setup: Selina begins her speech at the Iowa dinner
jokes.punchline: The single word 'Again.' — dropped as a flat acknowledgment of her repeated failure and return
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — one word that contains the entire Selina Meyer story. The pause before 'Again.' does all the work. Self-aware in a way that's rare for Selina and therefore funny.

jokes.text: from Palo Alto to Hollywood, from Iowa to New Hampshire, and from the Hamptons to Wall Street. I'm going to be with the real folks out there.
jokes.setup: Selina describes her upcoming 'feeling tour' to connect with real Americans
jokes.punchline: Every location she names is wealthy or coastal — Palo Alto, Hollywood, the Hamptons, Wall Street — while claiming to be with 'real folks'
jokes.explanation: A well-structured political hypocrisy joke — the list of 'real folks' locations is entirely elite. The character integration is excellent: Selina is constitutionally incapable of understanding what 'regular America' means.

jokes.text: I wanna feel their feelings and I wanna hear their speakings.
jokes.setup: Selina describes her populist tour goals
jokes.punchline: 'Hear their speakings' — a mangled, made-up gerund that reveals she doesn't actually speak the language of the people she claims to represent
jokes.explanation: 'Their speakings' is a perfect Selina mangled-language moment — it sounds like someone who has heard about regular people but never actually been one. The phoniness is encoded in the grammar. 'Speakings' as a word that doesn't exist captures the performative populism perfectly.

jokes.text: I could've gone anywhere. But I didn't. I chose the South Bronx so I could feel and smell what America was all about. It smells good!
jokes.setup: Selina claims she chose the South Bronx to connect with America
jokes.punchline: The crowd member's enthusiastic 'It smells good!' — defending the South Bronx while completely missing (or embracing) the condescension
jokes.explanation: Selina's condescending 'feel and smell' language about the South Bronx is good political satire, and the crowd's 'It smells good!' patriotic defense is a perfectly ambiguous response — is it defensive pride or oblivious agreement? Either way it's funny.

jokes.text: Washington insiders, they didn't like me very much. So let's send them a message by shoving the guy that they hate the most right back in their faces.
jokes.setup: Jonah gives his exploratory committee announcement speech
jokes.punchline: He positions his unpopularity as an anti-establishment virtue — 'the guy they hate the most' as his sales pitch
jokes.explanation: Jonah accidentally making a coherent populist argument while trying to spin universal hatred into a credential is sharp political satire. The joke is that he's not wrong — this is how modern anti-establishment politics works.

jokes.text: which basically means I'm definitely running. God bless New Hampshire. God bless the United States of America and Puerto Rico... if they can vote for president... And God bless Jonah Ryan.
jokes.setup: Jonah closes his announcement speech
jokes.punchline: Puerto Rico caveat ('if they can vote for president') signals he doesn't know Puerto Ricans can't vote in general elections; self-blessing at the end — 'God bless Jonah Ryan'
jokes.explanation: The Puerto Rico caveat is excellent political satire — he includes them 'if they can vote' revealing both his ignorance and his pandering. But 'God bless Jonah Ryan' is the standout moment: the narcissistic self-blessing that violates every convention of the political speech closer is perfectly Jonah. One of the episode's most memorable bits.

jokes.text: Okay, people, look, the president can only do so much without the cooperation of the Congress... I can't impress upon you enough that this process only works when there's bipartisan communication. That is what the Constitution requires, right? Okay, you in the back. Uh, will this be on the final?
jokes.setup: Selina is apparently teaching a civics class as part of her post-presidential activities
jokes.punchline: A student asks if it will be on the final — reducing Selina's hard-won political wisdom to exam material
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Selina is teaching civics (the humiliation of a former VP teaching a class) is good contextual comedy. The student's 'will this be on the final?' deflation is functional.

jokes.text: And if you wanna know something about government, watch this film 'Air Force One.' It's a good movie. Shows you what a president can do. Nice to meet you, and I'll see you guys Wednes... Tuesday.
jokes.setup: Selina wraps up her civics lesson
jokes.punchline: Recommending 'Air Force One' (an action movie) as civics education, then not knowing what day she'll next be there
jokes.explanation: Assigning 'Air Force One' as civics homework is peak Selina — the movie tells you what she thinks the presidency is (action heroism, personal drama) rather than what it actually involves. The day-of-week fumble is a good closing beat showing her disengagement from civilian schedules.

tmdb_overview: Selina and her team prepare for the long-awaited groundbreaking of her library. Amy seeks clarity on her role. Jonah asks a major donor for help. Season finale.
insight: Season finale escalates to 73 jokes in 47 minutes, driven by character chaos and dark political subversion.
